meta_notes: Both feeds rebuilt clean (250 signal videos, 40 stitch candidates of 187 rows). Underlying Apify extract is 2026-07-20T09:15Z — ~25h old, so every age skews ~1d conservative; no fallback needed. Virality sweep: 14 in-lane candidates cleared ≥1M views or ≥200K/day — 6 in the shortlist, 8 in more_stitches (cap extended from 4 per the guarantee), 1 of those an unidentified stub (@aqeelaaz_, 2.86M, 23.6K comments, caption 'our fav plushie' carries no claim). @kd_namjun (1.02M, K-drama) was the only ≥1M candidate excluded as out-of-lane noise. News shelf holds ONE verified item: the ITV story's date was confirmed from its visible dateline ('Friday 17 July 2026 at 6:41pm') and matches its meta timestamp. Three candidates were checked and dropped on story date: the J&J/Neutrogena benzene sunscreen recall is July 14 2021 (a search summary mis-stated it as 2026), the Connecticut AG–Sephora warning-label agreement is April 20 2026 (~92d), and the DermaRite recall expansion is an ICYMI of a July 2025 event. Bemotrizinol remains the June 9 story and stays out. Nothing padded. Tracked derm accounts above 1M (@dr.derm.love, @dermguru, @drspf, @drdrayzday) are excluded from stitching by the builder and surface as theme receipts instead. Run started 06:07, finished after the 6:30 cron at full quality — no time-budget degradation was applied.

themes.name: Diaper cream is the new barrier cream — and it already has a corrective
themes.receipt: 3 breakouts · 3 creators · 4.2M views · 6–75s winning · you posted into it yesterday

videos.author: dermguru
videos.caption: Comment BARRIER CREAM and I'll send you a few skincare options to heal your barrier! If diaper paste is intimidating and you're not ready to fully commit to the trend…

videos.author: drdrayzday
videos.caption: Diaper rash cream is not sunscreen. Always look for sunscreens that are labeled broad spectrum and with a stated SPF

videos.author: abby.waldmanmd
videos.caption: Zinc oxide diaper cream has been my secret barrier repair cream for the last 10 years. It quickly repairs red, irritated and painful skin…

her_coverage.summary: Yours went up 14h ago and is already a 5% breakout at 11.6K views/hr (IG) — and the corpus has backed this for years: barrier-repair guides (63.5K TT), the microneedling aftercare rec, and the tretinoin 'nights off' protocol all name a zinc/diaper barrier cream.

links.label: her zinc diaper cream post · 171K in 0.6d

themes.corpus_position: "If you have very sensitive skin it is okay to do home micro needling, but after you may just really want to stick to something simple like a barrier cream — you can use just everyday diaper cream."
themes.winning_length: 6s (yours) or 60–75s (dermguru's explainer) — the middle is dead

angles.angle: name the dose: which zinc %, how thick, how long
split it: barrier repair YES, sunscreen NO

stitch_shortlist.author: @itvnews
stitch_shortlist.claim: kids as young as 4 are using adult anti-ageing products

pitch.the_claim: An ITV News investigation found four-year-olds using retinol and acid products built for adult skin; the British Association of Dermatologists is asking for warning labels. A 13-year-old in the piece got a swollen rash that took months to heal.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 2.10M in 3.8d with 5,211 comments — comment volume is 2.5× the usual for this size, which is the controversy signal. It is a news peg, so it stays alive for days, and stitching a broadcaster punches sideways at the marketing, not at a creator.
pitch.your_fit: You have the teen-skincare guide (18.3K) and a full anti-aging guide, but nothing on the under-13 question — and you are the mother of an 8- and a 5-year-old. That is the credential nobody else stitching this has.
angles: empathy-first: the mom bought it because the packaging looked child-safe
'you want me to WHAT?': retinol on a five-year-old
switch-don't-quit: the three-step routine a kid actually needs

stitch_shortlist.author: @alim_kh0
stitch_shortlist.claim: zinc oxide as a full-face mask hack

pitch.the_claim: Slather zinc oxide paste on as a mask. 2.90M views and it is the civilian end of the same wave dermguru and you are both on this week.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Misses only on rising (5.7d). Everything else is clean: an explicit demonstration, dead centre of your lane, and it lands the day after your own zinc post — the algorithm already knows you for this.
pitch.your_fit: Directly corpus-grounded: you tell people to use everyday diaper cream as a barrier after microneedling and to take 'nights off' with a zinc-based barrier during tretinoin. You have the position; this gives you the demonstration to react to.
angles: empathy-first: right ingredient, wrong instructions
switch-don't-quit: keep the zinc, lose the occlusion time
the one skin type that should never do this

stitch_shortlist.author: @brookemonk_
stitch_shortlist.claim: Korean sunscreen serum is the sun protection upgrade

pitch.the_claim: A sponsored post (#RoundLabPartner) selling a Korean sunscreen serum as real sun protection. 2.83M in 1.5d — the fastest-moving in-lane video in the feed.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Scores 5/6 and the miss is worth stating out loud: it is an ad, so the rubric discounts it. But the underlying claim is a genuine derm question — serum-texture SPF and whether the K-filter advantage survives US shelves — and Brooke Monk is enormous, so this punches up at the marketing rather than at a person.
pitch.your_fit: You posted 'Why are US sunscreens so bad compared to European, Australian and Asian sunscreens? The FDA has strictly controlled sunscreen filters…' two days ago. This is that argument with 2.83M views attached to it.
angles: 'you want me to WHAT?': serum texture vs the 2mg/cm² you actually need
empathy-first: the K-filters really are better — here's the catch
what changes when bemotrizinol hits US shelves

stitch_shortlist.author: @sierrajarratt
stitch_shortlist.claim: "I bathe in tret, I slather that shit everywhere"

pitch.the_claim: Full-body, everywhere, every night tretinoin. Stated as a personality trait rather than a routine.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Misses only on rising. It is a specific dosing behaviour, it is genuinely risky for the barrier, and the comments are full of people asking whether they should copy it — which is the whole reason to answer.
pitch.your_fit: Your tretinoin beginners' guide (166.7K on YouTube) makes exactly the counter-argument: 'this is about the long haul game… take some nights off, use a zinc-based barrier.' A repeat needs a new angle, so lead with the barrier, not the frequency.
angles: empathy-first: nobody told you the retinoid uglies end
switch-don't-quit: same tube, three nights a week, sandwich method
where on the body tret genuinely should not go

stitch_shortlist.author: @drjessicaburgy
stitch_shortlist.claim: nail melanoma is a band, not a blotch

pitch.the_claim: Nail melanoma shows as a dark longitudinal band; a subungual hematoma is a red-purple bruise that grows out after trauma.
pitch.why_it_stitches: A fellow derm being correct, so this is a 'yes, and —' only. It earns the slot because 1.07M people just learned a rule with real exceptions, and you are the one who takes the biopsy.
pitch.your_fit: Your melanoma corpus is strong (the 'three things to ask your doctor' video, the 493K BCC guide) but nail-unit melanoma specifically is a gap — you have onychomycosis covered, not this.
angles: yes, and: Hutchinson's sign is the part that changes management
the band that's normal — skin of colour deserves its own rule
what the nail biopsy is actually like, from the surgeon

stitch_shortlist.author: @johnzademd
stitch_shortlist.claim: benzoyl peroxide wash for underarm odour

pitch.the_claim: Benzoyl peroxide wash a few times a week in the shower to cut odour-causing underarm bacteria.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Another derm being right — 'yes, and' framing required. It clears the bar on size and on a concrete, testable protocol people will try this week.
pitch.your_fit: You have the staph-colonisation tips post (83K) and deep acne corpus, so benzoyl peroxide dosing is second nature. Flag the bleached-shirt problem nobody warns about.
angles: yes, and: it bleaches every dark shirt you own
when odour is actually erythrasma and BP won't fix it
the 60-second version for people who won't add a step

more_stitches.author: @nygiants
more_stitches.claim: NFL player guesses beauty products blind

pitch.the_claim: Braxton correctly names a table of beauty products. 8.92M views at 2.4M/day — the biggest thing anywhere near this lane.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Lane call stated honestly: there is NO derm claim here, it is a locker-room bit. Surfaced because of size, not fit. If you want it, the play is the format (blind-guess the product) rather than a rebuttal.
pitch.your_fit: Nothing in the corpus to ground a response — this would be a format steal, not a react.
angles: steal the format: blind-guess which product is the actual actives

more_stitches.author: @bbyambi
more_stitches.claim: expensive things that are actually worth the money

pitch.the_claim: The civilian version of the derm splurge lists — 2.90M views, general 'expensive things worth it', only partly skincare.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Lane doubt stated: the list runs broader than skin. It is here because it is the mass-market end of your theme-2 format and shows the appetite is not derm-specific.
pitch.your_fit: Your pricey-picks video is the derm answer to exactly this, and your saving-money guide is the counterweight.
angles: react to only the skincare picks and score them
the one on her list you'd return

pitch.the_claim: A Nepali clinic marketing an azelaic acid / melasma ointment. 2.37M views.
pitch.why_it_stitches: In-lane and enormous, so the guarantee surfaces it — but it fails 'punches sideways or up': stitching a small overseas clinic punches down. Recommend leaving it unless you take the ingredient, not the clinic.
pitch.your_fit: You posted an azelaic acid community prompt yesterday (94K in 1.0d), so the ingredient is live for you right now.
angles: take the ingredient, never the clinic: what azelaic actually does for melasma
pitch.length: skip the stitch; make it a standalone

pitch.the_claim: Glycolic acid as a heel and body-care fix. 1.91M views.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Clean in-lane demonstration at scale; misses only on rising. Body-care acids are a lane you already teach.
pitch.your_fit: Your glycolic acid explainer (390K on IG) is explicit that AHAs belong 1–3×/week, which is the correction this needs.
angles: empathy-first: right acid, wrong frequency
urea beats glycolic on heels — here's why

more_stitches.author: @febisola_iris
more_stitches.claim: sun toner + cooling cream as sun protection

pitch.the_claim: A branded 'Sun Water' toner and sorbet cream framed as the hydrated-and-protected routine. 1.26M views.
pitch.why_it_stitches: In-lane with a real error to correct — a sun toner is not an SPF step. Sponsored, which discounts it, and it is 5.4d old.
pitch.your_fit: Straight from your sunscreen corpus on what does and does not count as protection.
angles: 'you want me to WHAT?': a toner is not a sunscreen
what 'sun water' actually is

pitch.the_claim: Sibling comedy wrapped around a Yesstyle discount code. 1.12M views.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Lane doubt stated plainly: no derm claim, it is a code drop. Surfaced only because it clears the size bar on a skincare seed.

pitch.the_claim: A named-product stack — Farmacy balm, La Roche-Posay milk cleanser, medicube zero-pore pads, mixsoon — as the pore routine. 578K at 216K/day.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Pacing above the guarantee threshold and still climbing at 2.7d. Named products mean a specific, checkable claim.
pitch.your_fit: Your sebaceous-filament and pore corpus covers why pore pads do not shrink pores.
angles: empathy-first: those are filaments, not clogs
which two of the four are actually doing work

more_stitches.author: @aqeelaaz_
more_stitches.claim: content unidentifiable from feed (no caption) — watch in-app first

news.headline: Children as young as four using adult anti-ageing skincare
news.summary: ITV News investigation: the British Association of Dermatologists wants warning labels; CTPA data says 61% of 8–14s use skincare and one in ten of those use anti-ageing products. A 13-year-old's reaction took months to heal.
news.her_angle: You have two daughters in exactly that age band — the under-13 routine video nobody credible has made.
